words.shortDefinition: Using HTML elements that convey the meaning of the content.
words.simpleDefinition: Using HTML elements for what they actually mean — headings for headings, buttons for buttons — so browsers and assistive tech understand the page's structure, not just its appearance.
words.longDefinition: The practice of using HTML tags for their intended purpose (e.g., <button> for actions, <nav> for navigation) rather than just for their visual appearance. This provides structural information that assistive technologies use to navigate the page.
words.techContext: The foundation of a11y. Screen readers use semantic tags to provide a list of landmarks or headings for the user to jump through.
words.exampleSentence: By using semantic HTML tags like <main> and <article>, we made our blog post much easier to navigate for screen reader users.
words.connector: Think of semantic HTML like labelled containers in a kitchen — using a 'flour' jar for flour and a 'sugar' jar for sugar rather than unlabelled tubs, so anyone can find what they need.

codeExample.code: Click me

words.shortDefinition: Attributes that define the type of an element when semantic HTML is insufficient.
words.simpleDefinition: Attributes you add to HTML elements to tell assistive technologies what role they play — 'this is a navigation menu', 'this is a dialog' — when HTML alone isn't descriptive enough.
words.longDefinition: Accessible Rich Internet Applications roles. They describe the purpose of an element to assistive technology, such as role='dialog' for a modal or role='tabpanel' for a tab interface.
words.techContext: Used when custom UI components (like a complex tree view or a dropdown) cannot be fully described by native HTML elements.
words.exampleSentence: We added role='alert' to the error message container so the screen reader would announce it immediately when it appeared.
words.etymology: Acronym for Accessible Rich Internet Applications.
words.connector: Think of ARIA roles like name badges at a conference — they tell a screen reader exactly who this element is and what they're there to do.

words.shortDefinition: The practice of controlling which element currently receives keyboard input.
words.simpleDefinition: Deliberately controlling where keyboard focus moves — especially when content changes, like opening a modal — so keyboard users always have a logical next target.
words.longDefinition: Ensuring that the user's keyboard focus moves logically through the application, especially when UI elements appear or disappear (like opening a modal or navigating to a new page).
words.techContext: Critical for keyboard and screen reader users. When a modal closes, focus should return to the button that opened it.
words.exampleSentence: We implemented focus management to ensure the user's cursor moved into the search input as soon as the search drawer opened.
words.etymology: from Latin focus ('hearth, center of interest').
words.connector: Think of focus management like a tour guide leading a group — when the tour moves to a new room, the guide makes sure everyone enters in an orderly way rather than scattering.

words.shortDefinition: Assistive software that converts on-screen text and elements into speech or braille.
words.simpleDefinition: Software that reads the content of a screen aloud or outputs it in Braille, letting people who can't see the display navigate and interact by listening.
words.longDefinition: A tool used primarily by people who are blind or have low vision to interact with digital content. It interprets the underlying code (DOM) and accessibility tree to describe the interface.
words.techContext: Developers test their apps with screen readers like VoiceOver (macOS/iOS), TalkBack (Android), or NVDA (Windows) to ensure a11y compliance.
words.exampleSentence: The screen reader didn't announce the status update because the container lacked an aria-live attribute.
words.connector: Think of a screen reader like an audio description track on a film — it narrates what's happening for someone who can't see the picture.

words.shortDefinition: A set of international standards for web accessibility.
words.simpleDefinition: Web Content Accessibility Guidelines — the internationally recognised standards that define how to make web content accessible to people with disabilities, organised into levels A, AA, and AAA.
words.longDefinition: Web Content Accessibility Guidelines. Developed by the W3C, these guidelines categorize accessibility into four principles (POUR): Perceivable, Operable, Understandable, and Robust.
words.techContext: Most legal requirements and corporate policies point to WCAG 2.1 Level AA as the baseline for compliance.
words.exampleSentence: Our accessibility audit revealed that our color scheme failed the WCAG 2.1 AA contrast requirements.
words.etymology: Acronym for Web Content Accessibility Guidelines.
words.connector: Think of WCAG like a building code for the web — just as construction codes set minimum safety standards for physical buildings, WCAG sets minimum accessibility standards for digital ones.

words.shortDefinition: The difference in luminance between text and its background.
words.simpleDefinition: A measure of how different the brightness of foreground text is from its background — a higher ratio means the text is easier to read for people with low vision.
words.longDefinition: A numerical value (e.g., 4.5:1) representing the brightness difference between foreground and background. High contrast is necessary for readability, especially for users with low vision or color blindness.
words.techContext: WCAG AA requires a contrast ratio of at least 4.5:1 for normal text and 3:1 for large text.
words.exampleSentence: I had to darken the light gray text to meet the 4.5:1 contrast ratio required for accessibility.
words.connector: Think of contrast ratio like writing on a whiteboard — black marker on white is easy to read (high contrast); yellow marker on white is almost invisible (low contrast).

words.shortDefinition: A text description of an image for users who cannot see it.
words.simpleDefinition: A short text description attached to an image so screen readers can convey what the image shows to users who can't see it.
words.longDefinition: Short for 'alternative text'. It is provided via the alt attribute on tags. It is read by screen readers and displayed if the image fails to load.
words.techContext: Crucial for both a11y and SEO. Decorative images should have an empty alt attribute (alt='') so they are ignored by screen readers.
words.exampleSentence: The alt text for the graph should describe the main trend, not just say 'a chart of data'.
words.connector: Think of alt text like a caption on a photo in a print magazine — it tells readers who can't see the image what's in it.

words.shortDefinition: Internal page links that allow users to jump past repetitive content.
words.simpleDefinition: Hidden links at the top of a page that let keyboard users jump straight to the main content, bypassing repeated navigation menus on every page.
words.longDefinition: Usually placed as the very first element in the DOM, skip links allow keyboard users to jump directly to the main content, bypassing long navigation menus.
words.techContext: Often hidden visually until they receive focus, at which point they appear at the top of the screen.
words.exampleSentence: We added a 'Skip to main content' link so keyboard users don't have to tab through twenty nav items on every page.
words.connector: Think of skip links like an express elevator button — you don't have to stop at every floor (nav item); you can go straight to where you're actually headed.

codeExample.code: Skip to content

words.shortDefinition: An attribute that tells screen readers to announce updates to an element.
words.simpleDefinition: An attribute that tells screen readers to automatically announce updates to a region of the page — like a new notification or a search result appearing — without the user having to move focus there.
words.longDefinition: Used to notify users of dynamic content changes that happen without a page reload, such as a countdown timer, a stock ticker, or a success message after submitting a form.
words.techContext: Values include 'polite' (waits for the screen reader to finish current speech) and 'assertive' (interrupts immediately).
words.exampleSentence: By setting aria-live='polite' on the notification toast, we ensured users were aware of the success message without being interrupted.
words.connector: Think of ARIA live like a ticker-tape announcement in an office — new information appears on the board and is read out automatically, no need for anyone to walk over and check.

words.shortDefinition: A mechanism that keeps keyboard focus within a specific element.
words.simpleDefinition: Keeping keyboard focus locked inside a component — like a modal dialog — so users can't accidentally tab behind it to an element they can't interact with.
words.longDefinition: Used primarily in modals or dialogs to ensure that when a user presses 'Tab', the focus cycles through the interactive elements of the modal and doesn't 'leak' back into the main page content behind it.
words.techContext: Essential for security and usability in dialogs. Prevents users from accidentally interacting with the 'hidden' page content.
words.exampleSentence: I used a focus trap library to ensure that keyboard users couldn't tab out of the modal until they either saved or closed it.
words.connector: Think of a focus trap like velvet ropes at a club VIP area — once you're inside (the modal), you stay inside until you're done; the rest of the venue is off-limits while the area is active.

words.shortDefinition: An attribute used to provide a string that labels an interactive element.
words.simpleDefinition: An attribute that provides a human-readable name for an element that a screen reader will announce, overriding or supplementing any visible text.
words.longDefinition: Provides a text string as an accessible name for an element. This is used when there is no visible text on the screen to provide a label (like an icon-only button).
words.techContext: Overwrites any existing text content for assistive technology. Use aria-labelledby if the label text already exists elsewhere on the page.
words.exampleSentence: Since the search button only contains a magnifying glass icon, I added an aria-label of 'Search site' for screen reader users.
words.connector: Think of ARIA label like an audio tag on a museum exhibit — even if there's no visible label on the artefact, the audio guide gives it a clear name when you point your device at it.

words.shortDefinition: HTML elements that define the major sections of a page.
words.simpleDefinition: HTML elements like <header>, <nav>, <main>, and <footer> that define the major regions of a page, letting screen reader users jump quickly between sections.
words.longDefinition: Sections like <header>, <nav>, <main>, <aside>, and <footer>. Screen readers provide a shortcut to jump between these landmarks, allowing users to quickly orient themselves on a new page.
words.techContext: A page should ideally have exactly one <main> landmark and at least one <nav> landmark if it has navigation.
words.exampleSentence: The accessibility audit suggested adding more landmark elements to help users quickly skip past the header to the main content.
words.etymology: from 'landmark' (a conspicuous object used to mark a locality).
words.connector: Think of landmark elements like the named sections on a tourist map — 'Old Town', 'Market Quarter', 'Harbour' — they let visitors skip straight to an area without walking the whole city.

words.shortDefinition: The ability to use a website using only a keyboard.
words.simpleDefinition: The ability to use a website or app entirely with a keyboard — tabbing between elements, pressing Enter to activate them — without ever needing a mouse.
words.longDefinition: Fundamental for users with motor disabilities. It involves using Tab to move between elements, Enter/Space to activate them, and Arrow keys for menus or sliders.
words.techContext: Interactive elements must be reachable via Tab and show a clear 'focus ring' when selected.
words.exampleSentence: We tested the new dropdown component to ensure that keyboard navigation worked as expected using the arrow keys.
words.connector: Think of keyboard navigation like operating an elevator with buttons — every action is available from a fixed set of controls, no pointing device required.

words.shortDefinition: The sequence in which elements receive focus when the user presses the Tab key.
words.simpleDefinition: The sequence in which keyboard focus moves through interactive elements on a page when the user presses Tab — ideally matching the visual and logical reading order.
words.longDefinition: By default, the tab order follows the order of elements in the DOM. This should match the visual reading order of the page to avoid confusing the user.
words.techContext: Using tabindex > 0 is considered an anti-pattern as it creates a disconnected and hard-to-maintain tab order.
words.exampleSentence: The tab order was jumping from the header to the footer because of some absolute positioning in our CSS, so we reordered the DOM nodes.
words.connector: Think of tab order like the reading order of a form — you expect to fill in Name, then Email, then Phone; if Tab jumps from Name to Phone and back, it's disorienting.

words.shortDefinition: An attribute that links an element to another that describes it.
words.simpleDefinition: An attribute that links an element to another element containing additional descriptive text, so a screen reader announces both the element and its longer description.
words.longDefinition: Identifies the element (or elements) that provide extra descriptive text for an object. This is often used to link a form input to its error message or help text.
words.techContext: Unlike aria-label (which provides a name), aria-describedby provides additional context. Both will be read by the screen reader.
words.exampleSentence: I linked the password input to the 'requirements' div using aria-describedby so users would hear the password rules as they type.
words.connector: Think of aria-describedby like a footnote reference in a document — the main text points to extra context so readers can choose to dig deeper.

codeExample.code: Minimum 8 characters.

words.shortDefinition: A CSS pseudo-class used to show focus indicators only for keyboard users.
words.simpleDefinition: The requirement that the currently focused element always has a visible indicator — like a highlight ring — so keyboard users can see where they are on the page.
words.longDefinition: It allows developers to show a focus ring when a user navigates via keyboard, but hide it when they use a mouse. This satisfies both accessibility needs and design preferences.
words.techContext: Replaces the old practice of setting 'outline: none', which was terrible for accessibility.
words.exampleSentence: By using :focus-visible, we kept the clean look for mouse users while providing a clear blue outline for keyboard navigators.
words.connector: Think of focus visible like the cursor on a screen — you always know where you are because there's a visible pointer; removing it would make navigation blind.

words.shortDefinition: An ARIA role that defines a region of the page.
words.simpleDefinition: An ARIA role that marks a section of a page as a named region — like 'navigation' or 'main' — giving screen reader users a quick way to jump between major areas.
words.longDefinition: Common roles include role='main', role='navigation', role='search', and role='banner'. These are equivalent to HTML5 tags like <main> and <nav>.
words.techContext: If you cannot use semantic HTML tags, you should use generic <div> or <section> tags with these ARIA roles to maintain accessibility.
words.exampleSentence: Since we were forced to use a legacy framework that only outputs <div> tags, we added role='navigation' to ensure the sidebar was accessible.
words.connector: Think of landmark roles like department signs in a hospital — 'Emergency', 'Radiology', 'Outpatients' — letting people go straight to the right area without reading every door.

words.shortDefinition: A feature that allows users to choose the size of textual content displayed on their device.
words.simpleDefinition: A system feature that lets users set a preferred text size and have apps automatically scale all text to match, so people who need larger text don't have to squint.
words.longDefinition: Mainly used in mobile OSs (iOS/Android), it allows users to scale font sizes globally. Apps must be built to respond to these changes by scaling their UI layouts without cutting off text.
words.techContext: Developers should use relative units (like 'rem' or 'em') instead of fixed 'px' to ensure their web apps respect the user's browser font size settings.
words.exampleSentence: Our layout broke when we tested it with Dynamic Type set to the largest setting, so we had to switch our heights from pixels to auto.
words.connector: Think of Dynamic Type like adjustable font size in an e-reader — you turn one dial and the whole book resizes to whatever's comfortable for your eyes.

words.shortDefinition: The technical specification that defines how to make web content more accessible.
words.simpleDefinition: A specification that defines extra attributes developers can add to HTML to communicate structure and behaviour to assistive technologies when native HTML isn't expressive enough.
words.longDefinition: Web Accessibility Initiative - Accessible Rich Internet Applications. It's a suite of attributes that help make complex widgets (like tree views, drag-and-drop, or live updates) accessible.
words.techContext: The gold standard for creating accessible web components that go beyond simple static pages.
words.exampleSentence: Implementing the WAI-ARIA design patterns helped us build a fully accessible custom date picker from scratch.
words.etymology: Acronym for Web Accessibility Initiative - Accessible Rich Internet Applications.
words.connector: Think of WAI-ARIA like subtitles for an interface — it provides a layer of description that helps screen readers narrate what's happening for people who can't see it directly.

words.shortDefinition: The built-in screen reader for Apple's operating systems.
words.simpleDefinition: Apple's built-in screen reader on iOS and macOS that reads screen content aloud and lets users navigate interfaces using gestures or keyboard commands.
words.longDefinition: Included in macOS, iOS, iPadOS, and watchOS. It allows blind and low-vision users to control their devices using gestures and keyboard commands while hearing a description of the screen.
words.techContext: Since many developers use MacBooks and iPhones, VoiceOver is often the first screen reader they use for a11y testing.
words.exampleSentence: I used VoiceOver on my iPhone to verify that the checkout button was properly announced as 'Double tap to pay'.
words.connector: Think of VoiceOver like an audio tour at a museum — it narrates everything on display so visitors who can't read the labels still get the full experience.

words.shortDefinition: A text version of the spoken part of a video, often including non-speech information.
words.simpleDefinition: Text displayed on screen that transcribes the spoken dialogue and relevant sounds in a video, making audio content accessible to people who are deaf or hard of hearing.
words.longDefinition: Unlike subtitles (which only translate dialogue), captions include descriptions of sound effects, music, and speaker identification to provide a full experience for deaf or hard-of-hearing users.
words.techContext: Usually provided via .vtt (WebVTT) or .srt files and linked using the <track> tag in HTML5 video.
words.exampleSentence: We added closed captions to our product demo video to make it accessible to users with hearing impairments.
words.connector: Think of captions like the words on a silent cinema title card — they translate what's spoken into something visible so nobody is excluded from the story.

codeExample.code:
<video controls>
  <source src="video.mp4" type="video/mp4">
  <track label="English" kind="captions" srclang="en" src="captions.vtt" default>
</video>

words.shortDefinition: A text-based version of audio or video content.
words.simpleDefinition: A complete written version of the audio (and sometimes visual) content of a video or podcast, available separately so users can read rather than listen.
words.longDefinition: A full text document that includes everything spoken in an audio file or video, often including descriptions of visual actions. They are essential for accessibility and also improve SEO.
words.techContext: Should be provided alongside any podcast or video content as a fallback for users who cannot or prefer not to use audio/video.
words.exampleSentence: Providing a full transcript of the keynote speech allowed users to search and read the content at their own pace.
words.connector: Think of a transcript like the text version of a radio programme — the full conversation in readable form, so you can search it, read at your own pace, or share a quote.

words.shortDefinition: The use of touch or vibration to communicate with the user.
words.simpleDefinition: Physical vibrations or taps from a device that communicate information through touch — confirming a button press or alerting the user to an event.
words.longDefinition: Used in mobile apps to provide non-visual and non-auditory confirmation of an action, such as a gentle vibration when a form is submitted successfully or a distinct pulse when an error occurs.
words.techContext: Part of a 'multimodal' accessibility strategy, ensuring the app communicates via sight, sound, and touch.
words.exampleSentence: We added a subtle haptic pulse to the 'Success' button to give users a physical sense of completion.
words.connector: Think of haptic feedback like a gentle tap on the shoulder — a physical signal that something happened, without needing to look at the screen or hear a sound.

words.shortDefinition: A reduced ability to distinguish between certain colors.
words.simpleDefinition: A condition affecting how people perceive colour — most commonly difficulty distinguishing red from green — that means design can't rely on colour alone to convey information.
words.longDefinition: Common types include Deuteranopia (green-weak) and Protanopia (red-weak). Accessible designs should never use color alone to convey meaning (e.g., using both an icon and color for errors).
words.techContext: Designers use simulators to check if their 'Success' and 'Error' states are distinguishable for all users.
words.exampleSentence: To accommodate users with color blindness, we added checkmark and X icons to our status indicators instead of just relying on green and red circles.
words.connector: Think of designing for colour blindness like annotating a map with both colour and pattern — people who can't distinguish the colours can still tell regions apart by the texture.

words.shortDefinition: The four foundational principles of the WCAG accessibility guidelines.
words.simpleDefinition: The four principles underpinning WCAG: Perceivable, Operable, Understandable, Robust — a checklist for whether any user can perceive, navigate, understand, and reliably use your content.
words.longDefinition: Perceivable (can be seen/heard), Operable (can be used with different inputs), Understandable (clear language/UI), and Robust (works across many devices/browsers).
words.techContext: The mental framework used when conducting an accessibility audit of an application.
words.exampleSentence: Our site is perceivable and understandable, but we need to work on making it more operable for keyboard-only users to meet the POUR principles.
words.etymology: Acronym for Perceivable, Operable, Understandable, Robust.
words.connector: Think of POUR like the four legs of a chair — remove any one and the whole thing falls over; all four must be solid for the product to be truly accessible.
